target: detail_verdict_text on WIN-035, WIN-036, WIN-037, WIN-038, WIN-039: replace D-template post-hoc sentence with OTS-specific variant that correctly addresses OpenTimestamps claims
context: The EXP-054 batch applied a D-template post-hoc sentence to all dataset-sourced WINs. For most WINs the sentence is correct: the monitor script hardcodes the target, so temporal ordering cannot be verified from the repository. But WIN-035 through WIN-039 are the dome's self-declared PROSPECTIVE WINs with OpenTimestamps blockchain anchoring. For these 5, the D-template claim that temporal ordering "cannot be checked from the repository history" is factually wrong — the OTS chain does provide verifiable timing. The correct argument is that OTS timestamps status_history.json (which bundles predictions AND observations in a single file), so the hash proves when the bundled file existed, not when the prediction preceded the observation. Additionally, the underlying data was publicly available years before registration for 4 of 5 WINs. EXP-068 (SHA-256 recycling section) has now been integrated, providing the Section 6 cross-reference needed.

WIN-035.current_first_paragraph: Non-discriminating: both models extrapolate the same station data. The dome's SAA 'prediction' is trend-following, not a derivation from dome geometry.
WIN-035.current_posthoc_paragraph: Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The confirming value comes from a field-specific published dataset (IGRF, NASA station archives, ocean-tide gauges, telluric records, etc.) that was publicly available at the time of the dome monitor commit, and we could not locate a pre-registration of the expected value in a timestamping venue independent of the author. Under the dome author's own definition ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"), the monitor script hardcodes the target number on the same commit as the data-fetch line, so the temporal ordering the definition requires cannot be checked from the repository history. The legitimate-novelty carve-out of Part 3.1b requires that the data be "not plausibly known to the author at write-time," which is not the case for indexed public datasets.
WIN-035.replacement_posthoc_paragraph: Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The dome claims prospective status via OpenTimestamps blockchain anchoring — and the OTS infrastructure is real: it provides a verifiable timestamp chain anchored to the Bitcoin blockchain. However, OTS timestamps status_history.json, which bundles prediction parameters together with observation results in a single file. The hash proves when the bundled file existed — not that the prediction preceded the observation. A genuine prospective timestamp would hash an isolated prediction document separately, before any confirming data is added. See Timestamp Error for the full analysis. Additionally, the SAA African cell decay rate (~28–30 nT/yr) was published in WMM/IGRF data years before PROS-005 was registered on 1 January 2026 — the Part 3.1b standard requires that the data be "not plausibly known to the author at write-time," which is not the case here.

WIN-036.replacement_posthoc_paragraph: Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The dome claims prospective status via OpenTimestamps blockchain anchoring — and the OTS infrastructure is real: it provides a verifiable timestamp chain anchored to the Bitcoin blockchain. However, OTS timestamps status_history.json, which bundles prediction parameters together with observation results in a single file. The hash proves when the bundled file existed — not that the prediction preceded the observation. See Timestamp Error. Additionally, WMM2025 (released December 2024) published the pole at ~139.3°E — over 14 months before PROS-006 was registered on 6 March 2026. This WIN is also logically entailed by WIN-007: a pole at 139.3°E is automatically 19.3° from 120°E. The Part 3.1b standard requires data "not plausibly known to the author at write-time," which is not the case for published WMM positions.

WIN-037.replacement_posthoc_paragraph: Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim raises post-hoc concerns despite having a genuinely prospective test window. The dome claims prospective status via OpenTimestamps blockchain anchoring, and for this WIN, the registration date (1 January 2026) plausibly precedes the March 2025–March 2026 measurement window. Credit where due: this is the closest any dome prediction comes to genuine temporal priority. However, OTS timestamps status_history.json, which bundles prediction parameters together with observation results in a single file (see Timestamp Error), and the predicted threshold of ≥28 nT matches the SAA's established decay rate (~28–30 nT/yr at Tsumeb, per NCEI/WMM data) — it is the model's own input parameter being confirmed by the same ongoing trend it was fitted to. Even granting the timing, this is a non-discriminating extrapolation: both dome and WMM2025 predict the same continued decay from the same station data.

WIN-038.replacement_posthoc_paragraph: Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The dome claims prospective status via OpenTimestamps blockchain anchoring — and the OTS infrastructure is real. However, OTS timestamps status_history.json, which bundles prediction parameters together with observation results in a single file (see Timestamp Error). The Schumann fundamental resonance at 7.83 Hz was published by Schumann in 1952 and has been continuously measured since the 1960s (HeartMath GCI; Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002). Registering the claim that 7.83 Hz "remains stable" 74 years after its discovery and timestamping it with OTS does not make it a prediction — it makes it a crypto-wrapped textbook fact. The Part 3.1b standard requires data "not plausibly known to the author at write-time," which is not the case for a 74-year-old measurement.

WIN-039.replacement_posthoc_paragraph: Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The dome claims prospective status via OpenTimestamps blockchain anchoring — and the OTS infrastructure is real. However, OTS timestamps status_history.json, which bundles prediction parameters together with observation results in a single file (see Timestamp Error). The 1–2 nT lunar magnetic signal was published by Tyler et al. (2003) in Science and refined by Sabaka et al. (2015) using Swarm satellite data — over 20 years before the dome model claimed it as a prediction. The dome's own predicted value (W015: −1.0 nT ±0.5) disagrees in sign with its claimed confirmation (1–2 nT positive), suggesting the value was adopted from literature without understanding the oscillating nature of the signal. The Part 3.1b standard requires data "not plausibly known to the author at write-time," which is not the case for measurements published two decades prior.

holes_addressed: EXP-054-batch.c2 moderate hole: D-template claims temporal ordering cannot be checked — factually wrong for OTS-anchored WINs 035-039
ISS-707: OTS gap for self-declared prospective WINs
new_evidence_added: OTS timestamps status_history.json (bundled predictions+observations), not isolated prediction documents
Per-WIN data availability timelines: WMM2025 Dec 2024 (WIN-036), Schumann 1952 (WIN-038), Tyler et al. 2003 (WIN-039), SAA trend decades old (WIN-035)
WIN-037 acknowledged as closest to genuine temporal priority — but non-discriminating extrapolation

anticipated_objections.objection: OTS provides verifiable timing and you are dismissing it
anticipated_objections.response: We explicitly acknowledge the OTS infrastructure is real and provides verifiable timing. Our argument is about WHAT is timestamped (a bundled file containing both predictions and observations) and WHETHER the data was already known (it was, for 4 of 5 WINs). We credit WIN-037 as the closest to genuine temporal priority.

anticipated_objections.objection: The git commit SHA also proves when the prediction was made
anticipated_objections.response: Git commits prove when code was committed to a specific repository, but the author controls the repository. OTS blockchain anchoring is stronger than git alone — which is why we engage with the OTS claim specifically rather than dismissing it. The problem is the bundled file, not the blockchain.

anticipated_objections.objection: You are being inconsistent — acknowledging OTS for WIN-037 but dismissing it for the others
anticipated_objections.response: We apply the same standard to all 5: OTS proves file timing but not prediction-observation separation (bundled file problem). The difference is that WIN-037 has a genuinely post-registration test window, while the other 4 predict values that were already published. Even for WIN-037, the prediction is non-discriminating because it extrapolates the same trend WMM uses.

anticipated_objections.objection: The dome author could separate the prediction and observation files, fixing the bundling issue
anticipated_objections.response: That would be a genuine methodological improvement — and exactly what we recommend in the Timestamp Error tab. If the author implements this separation, it strengthens the provenance claim (though the data-availability problem remains for retrodictions). This is the canary we are monitoring.

kernel_of_truth: The dome author has built genuinely competent OTS infrastructure. Bitcoin blockchain anchoring is cryptographically stronger than git-only timestamps and represents more methodological rigor than most alternative cosmologies attempt. For WIN-037 specifically, the test window (March 2025-2026) plausibly post-dates the January 2026 registration, making it the closest any dome prediction comes to genuine temporal priority. We must be honest about this even as we show why it still fails on non-discrimination grounds.
integration_notes: The decider should replace the second paragraph of detail_verdict_text for each of WIN-035 through WIN-039 using the per_win_replacements above. The first paragraph (scientific critique) is unchanged. The detail_extra timestamp notes can remain as-is — they provide a brief pointer to the Timestamp Error tab and are consistent with the new post-hoc text.
